response:
## Verdicts

**1. `route_metadata_drift` (crime_00014) → WARN, escalate to BLOCK if PR touches `/settings/billing`**

Four competing labels for one destination (file `billing.tsx`, component `PricingPage`, metadata.title `Plans`, `<title>` `Subscription`, nav labels `Plans`/`Billing`) is exactly the agent-risk pattern this charge is designed to catch — an agent renaming "Subscription" in the `<title>` will leave the other four out of sync, and there's no single source of truth to grep against. Doesn't break runtime, so don't block a drive-by PR on it. But any PR that edits `src/routes/settings/billing.tsx`, either nav file, or page chrome should block until the canonical name is picked and propagated. Agent risk 0.56 understates this because the `related_files` link straight to finding #12.

**2. `duplicated_navigation_source` (crime_00012) → WARN**

The same destination labelled "Plans" in `registry.ts` and "Billing" in `sidebar.ts` is a user-visible inconsistency (different labels in different nav surfaces for the same page) and a maintenance trap — an agent updating one will leave the other stale. But this is the same underlying problem as #14 and should be triaged together; flagging both as block would double-charge the PR for one fix. Warn now, with the expectation that fixing #14 fixes this. Don't ignore: confidence is 0.7 and the evidence is unambiguous.

**3. `docs_code_drift` (crime_00015) → IGNORE on most PRs, WARN if the PR touches `docs/teams.md`**

One broken relative link in a single doc page. High confidence (0.9) that it's actually broken, but low severity (0.35) and zero runtime impact — the worst case is an agent reading the docs and hitting a 404. Not worth gating a merge over. If the PR being reviewed is modifying `docs/teams.md` itself, surface it as a warn so the author fixes it in the same change; otherwise it's noise relative to findings #14 and #12.

## Note on the cluster

These three findings overlap and should be reviewed together rather than treated as independent items: #14 and #12 are two views of the same `/settings/billing` mess (the `related_files` cross-reference makes this explicit), and #15 is a much smaller separate hygiene item. A reviewer should land one fix for the billing destination and one for the broken link, not three separate "compliance" PRs.
